summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – EX TEMPORE – Application for Review of senior judicial registrar’s decision in relation to subpoena issued to Non Party Objector - Where the Application for Review seeks leave to proceed out of time - Consideration of the term "party" in r 14.04 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“Rules”) - Joinder of the Non Party Objector to proceedings on limited terms under r 3.04 - Where the Court deems the Application for Review to be an Application in a Proceeding pursuant to r 3.04 - No dispute service of the subpoena had not been effected in accordance with the Rules - Where the Court is satisfied the subpoena issued to the Non Party Objector be struck out - Balance of the Application in a Proceeding stood over - Partial costs order in the Non Party Objector’s favour.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

There is listed before me on 4 February 2026 what is described as an Application for Review of a decision of a judicial registrar made on 23 December 2025 in relation to subpoenas issued to the applicant, Ms Adi, by the second respondent to the proceedings, who is the husband between the spouse parties.
Ms Adi is not a party to the proceedings. She is rather an acknowledged romantic interest of the son of the husband and wife who is a respondent in the substantive proceedings.
She was nominated as the recipient of a subpoena issued by the Court on 8 December 2025. According to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“Rules”) the last date for service of that subpoena upon her was 12 December 2025.
When the matter came before me on 4 February 2026, there was no dispute that service had not been effected in accordance with the Rules by that date.
The husband, issuer of the subpoena, purported to serve Ms Adi at an address nominated in Suburb E, being D Street, Suburb E NSW. The evidence of Ms Adi is that she has never used that residence as her address and it was conceded by counsel for the issuer of the subpoena that service at this address was based upon what now turns out to be an incorrect assumption.
The relevant rules concerning service of a subpoena are quite clear that ordinary service not personal service is required and there is no dispute that this was not effected.
I note here that as part of the Application for Review, further submissions were made concerning the schedule to the subpoena challenging its breadth and questioning the apparent relevance of documents purported to be sought for production in accordance with the subpoena. For the following reasons I am not persuaded it is necessary to address those matters at this stage.
Senior Counsel for Ms Adi helpfully produced a detailed chronology of events which demonstrate the following.
After issuance of subpoenas and the passing of the last date for service on 12 December 2025, Ms Adi received notification on Tuesday 16 December 2025 of the existence of the subpoena by the son of the spouse parties. On the same day, 16 December 2025, Ms Adi instructed solicitors to, no doubt, give advice and represent her in relation to the subpoena.
Those solicitors sought from the solicitors for the husband copies of the subpoena addressed to her personally and additional subpoenas, to which I will refer, addressed to financial institutions.
Copies of the subpoenas were provided by the solicitors for the husband on 19 December 2025 at 5.36 pm. At 6.30 pm on the same day, Notices of Objection were sought to be filed in the registry on behalf of Ms Adi. Unsealed copies of the Notices of Objection were then sent to parties in the proceedings. The 19th of December 2025 was a Friday. By reason of the passing of the last time on that date for the filing of Notice of Objections, they were deemed by the Rules to be filed the next business day which was 22 December 2025, which was also the last day for production of any documents in compliance with the subpoenas.
In those circumstances, the senior judicial registrar on 23 December 2025 dismissed Ms Adi’s Notices of Objection in relation to all the subpoenas.
As part of the orders of the senior judicial registrar, service on Ms Adi of those orders was required, but that did not take place until 13 January 2026, which was also the last day of the 21 day period for filing an Application for Review from orders made by a registrar, in accordance with the Rules (r 14.05(1).
In those circumstances, when Ms Adi filed her Application for Review on 13 January 2026 at 6.47 pm she was, according to the Rules, several hours out of time.
Accordingly, the Application for Review was refused by the registry. A subsequent Application for Review was filed seeking leave to proceed out of time pursuant to r 15.06 of the Rules.
In those circumstances, I would have been prepared to extend the time for the Application for Review. However, a further potential difficulty lies in the wording of r 14.04 which gives standing to a “party” to apply for a review of an exercise of power by a senior judicial registrar.
“Party” is defined in r 1.05 inclusively to include the following:
(a) an applicant in a proceeding;

(b) an appellant in an appeal;

(c) a respondent to an application or appeal;

(d) an intervener in a proceeding.
On its face it does not seem to include a non-party who is a recipient of a subpoena.
When this matter was raised with senior counsel for Ms Adi, he submitted that “party” in r 14.04 should be construed to embrace a person adversely affected by an exercise of power by a senior judicial registrar. I am presently not attracted to that construction and in the short time available to consider the position no authority was cited in support of it. However, I do not consider it necessary to express a concluded view.
This is because r 3.04 provides that a person may be joined as a party to proceedings on limited terms. In circumstances where I am satisfied that there is no basis upon which the subpoena addressed to Ms Adi personally was properly served, it should be struck out irrespective of the position regarding any Notices of Objection.
Accordingly, to avoid any potential irregularity, it is appropriate in my view for Ms Adi to be joined to the proceedings on limited terms for the sole purpose of dealing with the subpoena purportedly addressed to her and properly served.
I take the view that it is open pursuant to the case management provisions in the Rules for me to deem her Application for Review to be an Application in a Proceeding pursuant to r 3.04 for that limited purpose, and I will make orders to that effect.
That leaves for consideration a number of subpoenas issued to a range of financial institutions which are also the subject of the Application for Review.
Ms Adi challenges paragraph 2 of the schedules to each of those subpoenas on the basis that paragraph is in the same terms as paragraph 2 of the schedule to the subpoena addressed to her.
It may be that they all suffer the same vice by reference to the well tilled field of authority concerning the breadth of schedules to subpoenas and questions of apparent relevance. However, I do not propose to delve into those matters for the purpose of this judgment.
Issues about service in relation to the subpoenas addressed to the financial institutions are not matters upon which Ms Adi can speak. No Notices of Objection by the financial institutions have been filed and it remains for Ms Adi to prosecute her arguments concerning the alleged vices in the schedules to those subpoenas at a properly constituted hearing having deemed her Application for Review to be an Application in a Proceeding on the limited terms to which I have already adverted to for that purpose.
Counsel for the husband also made clear that in the strange circumstances in which this matter came before me today, that his client was not in a position to adequately address arguments concerning the alleged vices in paragraph 2 of the subpoenas addressed to the financial institutions and he required time to put on material responsive to that filed and served by Ms Adi.
There are already Notices of Objection to other subpoenas currently listed for hearing before a senior judicial registrar on 20 April 2026, which raise some overlapping issues. In my view, it is convenient for the balance of Ms Adi’s objection to be stood over for determination as part of that hearing where it is plain that similar issues will be raised for consideration.
The applicant applies for a costs order in her favour.
It is true that she achieved partial success in relation to one subpoena at the hearing on 4 February 2026.
Section 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) provides that each party should pay their own costs. However, the applicant Ms Adi is not a party to proceedings, she is a third party objector to subpoenas.
It may be that she will ultimately succeed in relation to the remaining subpoenas which will be the subject of the senior judicial registrar’s consideration on 20 April 2026. However, I can form no view about that.
Guided by the provisions of s 114UB I have formed a view that the partial success of Ms Adi is a justifying circumstance for at least a partial costs order in her favour. It may be that before the senior judicial registrar she will achieve success, or she may fail, in which case there may be some costs consequences for her.
I consider it appropriate to deal with the question of costs now as opposed to reserving them.
In the circumstances I am satisfied it is appropriate to make an order that the second respondent pay 25 per cent of the costs of Ms Adi in relation to the hearing on 4 February 2026, as agreed or assessed. I direct that any assessment in accordance with these orders is not to take place until final determination of the proceedings.
